title: Te Whakamahi Hangarau mō te Whakahaumanu o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Technology for the Restoration of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Technology
culturalContext: Students will engage with the history and current state of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant local waterway that has been central to Ngāti Kahungunu for generations. The stream's name references the kotukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once flourished along its banks. Students will learn from local kaumātua about the stream's traditional uses for mahinga kai (food gathering) and its spiritual significance, while addressing modern challenges like water quality and biodiversity loss through technological solutions.
learningObjectives: Design and prototype a technological tool (e.g., water testing device, digital story map) that addresses a specific need identified for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Analyze and present data on water quality or biodiversity, connecting findings to the principles of kaitiakitanga (guardianship) and the health of the awa.
Collaborate effectively with peers and community experts to incorporate mātauranga Māori (Māori knowledge) and scientific data into a proposed technological solution.

activities.title: Whakatau me te Haerenga: Karakia ki te Awa (Welcome and Field Trip: Karakia to the Stream)
activities.description: The lesson begins with a pōwhiri (welcome) from a local kaumātua, who will share pūrākau (stories) of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. The class will then visit the awa to perform a karakia (prayer/incantation), observe its current state, and begin initial data collection (e.g., photos, sketches, simple water clarity tests).

activities.title: He Kohikohi Kōrero: Uiui me ngā Kaipūtaiao (Gathering Knowledge: Interviews with Scientists)
activities.description: Students will interview a local environmental scientist from the Hawke's Bay Regional Council and a kaumātua to understand both the mātauranga Māori and scientific data related to the health of the awa. They will use this knowledge to define a specific technological problem to solve.

activities.title: Te Hoahoa Hangarau: Te Waihanga me te Whakamātautau (Technology Design: Building and Testing)
activities.description: In the school's technology suite, students will work in small groups to design and build a functional prototype. This could be a physical model of a riparian planting tool, a digital dashboard for monitoring water quality data, or an interactive story map using ArcGIS Online to tell the story of the awa.

activities.title: Te Whakaaturanga me te Koha: Hoki ki te Awa (Presentation and Gift: Returning to the Stream)
activities.description: Groups will present their technological prototypes and findings to the class, the interviewed experts, and other community members. The final activity is a return to the awa to present the work—a digital portfolio, a physical model, or planted seedlings—as a koha (gift) and a tangible commitment to its restoration.

resources: Hawke's Bay Regional Council's Land Management Team (for water quality testing kits and expert guidance)
Local Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua (e.g., a kuia or koroua from the local marae)
ArcGIS Online or Google My Maps for creating digital story maps
The school's technology suite with materials for prototyping (e.g., micro:bits, sensors, recycled materials, 3D printer)

tasks: A digital portfolio documenting the entire design process, from initial research and interviews to the final prototype.
A 5-minute group presentation explaining the prototype, how it addresses a need of the awa, and how it incorporates both mātauranga Māori and scientific data.
A written individual reflection on their personal understanding of kaitiakitanga and the role of technology in supporting it.

nzcAlignment: Technology: Technological Practice (Level 4 - brief development, planning for practice, outcome development and evaluation)
Technology: Nature of Technology (Level 4 - characteristics of technology, characteristics of technological outcomes)
Social Sciences: Place and Environment (Level 4 - understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges)
The Principles: Cultural Diversity, Inclusion, and the Treaty of Waitangi
